behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score
behaviors.justification: The core behavior is a filtering operation that can be tested with mocked product catalog data. A unit test can directly verify the filter removes out-of-stock products. The MOCK-MISS risk is low: a mock catalog with stock status fields won't hide filtering logic errors. Real failures in this behavior manifest as out-of-stock products appearing in results—caught by testing the filter function directly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock product catalog with mixed in-stock and out-of-stock products across various relevance scores. Call the recommendation filter with this data. Assert that no out-of-stock products appear in the result set, regardless of their calculated relevance scores.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings
behaviors.justification: This is a straightforward filtering operation similar to behavior 1. User preferences (blocked categories) can be mocked directly. A unit test won't hide filtering logic errors—if the algorithm fails to exclude blocked categories, it will fail regardless of whether the settings come from a mock or a database.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock user with specific blocked categories (e.g., 'Electronics', 'Furniture') and a mock catalog spanning multiple categories. Call the recommendation filter. Assert that no products from blocked categories appear in the results while products from allowed categories are retained.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal
behaviors.justification: Ranking logic is an algorithmic operation. A unit test can create mock browsing history and product catalog, then verify relative ranking order. A mock doesn't hide ranking logic errors—if the algorithm fails to weight browsing patterns, it will fail with any data source (mock or real).
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with mocked browsing history for specific product categories. Generate recommendations and verify that products matching those categories rank higher than products with no browsing signal. Test with multiple user profiles to ensure consistent ranking behavior.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering—requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed
behaviors.justification: Pagination and sorting are deterministic algorithmic operations. A unit test can make two identical pagination requests on mocked data and compare results. The stability guarantee depends only on algorithm behavior, not I/O or external systems. Determinism issues in the algorithm itself are caught at unit level.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock ranked product list. Request page 2 twice without intervening state changes. Assert both requests return identical products in identical order. Verify pagination boundaries (page 1, middle pages, last page) return correct result counts and positions.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble
behaviors.justification: This is an algorithmic diversity requirement. A unit test can verify the recommendation results include products from unbrowsed categories by mocking user browsing history and verifying the output. The mock doesn't hide failures in the diversity logic—if the algorithm fails to surface unexpected categories, that failure occurs with any data source.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history limited to 3 specific categories. Generate recommendations and assert that at least one recommended product is from a category outside those 3. Test with multiple users and different browsing patterns to confirm consistent diversity behavior.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set
behaviors.justification: This is a conditional fallback logic: if history is empty, use popularity ranking. A unit test can create a mock new user (empty history) and verify recommendations are returned and popularity-ranked. The fallback logic doesn't require real data or I/O to test—if the code fails to trigger the fallback or ranks incorrectly, the unit test catches it.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock user with zero purchase history and zero browsing history. Call the recommendation engine. Assert that: (1) a non-empty result set is returned, (2) results are ordered by overall product popularity metrics, (3) all results are in-stock and match general (non-personalized) filters.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with
behaviors.justification: Click tracking involves both data formatting and persistence. A unit test can verify data structure formatting in isolation, but to ensure data is actually stored correctly we need a real storage mechanism (database, event log, or in-memory store). MOCK-MISS: a pure mocked database might not catch schema mismatches, serialization errors, or type conversion failures. The real failure mode—data persisted incorrectly or incompletely—requires testing against actual storage.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock recommendation response with known products and positions. Simulate a user click on a specific product at a specific position. Assert that the click event is persisted in a test database with exact product ID, position index, and timestamp within acceptable tolerance (±1 second). Verify multiple clicks for the same user are tracked independently and all data is retrievable.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance requirements demand testing at realistic data scale. A unit test with small mock datasets (10–100 products) might show sub-millisecond performance, completely hiding O(n²) or O(n log n) algorithmic inefficiencies that manifest at scale. REAL-INFRA: performance depends on actual data structure sizes, memory layout, and I/O patterns. MOCK-MISS: small mock data masks real slowdowns. The real failure—timeout at production scale—is only caught with realistic data volume.
behaviors.test_description: Load a test catalog of 100,000 products with realistic metadata, pricing, category hierarchies, and stock statuses. Generate recommendations for diverse user types: new user, active user with 6-month history, user with many blocked categories. Measure end-to-end latency for each. Assert all requests complete within 200ms. Optionally test concurrent requests (5–10 simultaneous) to verify timing holds under light load.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: This is primarily an algorithmic state-change and ranking behavior. A unit test can: (1) generate recommendations for a user, (2) update the user's purchase history in-process, (3) generate recommendations again, (4) verify the purchased product ranks lower or is excluded. The core failure—deprioritization logic not activating—is caught at unit level. Integration testing becomes important only if purchases arrive via external event streams and synchronization fails.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock user and generate initial recommendations. Record the rank of a specific product. Simulate a purchase by adding that product to the user's purchase history. Generate new recommendations. Assert the purchased product ranks significantly lower (or is excluded entirely) in the new list. Test with multiple purchases in a single request to verify behavior under concurrent purchase scenario.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state—requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list
behaviors.justification: Determinism is an algorithmic property. A unit test can make two identical requests with identical state and compare byte-for-byte output. The test will expose hidden non-determinism (random seeding, hash iteration order, floating-point rounding inconsistencies, etc.). MOCK-MISS risk is low: if the algorithm uses non-deterministic constructs, a unit test catches it. Integration testing becomes important only if non-determinism comes from concurrent state mutations.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user profile with fixed state (browsing history, preferences, purchase history). Call the recommendation function twice with identical inputs and no intervening operations. Assert the two result sets are byte-for-byte identical. Repeat with 5–10 different user profiles (new user, active user, user with varied preferences). Verify all algorithm components use seeded randomness (if any) rather than true random.
